title: Managing ducks for egg production
subtitle: Daily routines, housing, and feed for a small laying duck flock.
excerpt: Duck eggs weigh half as much again as a large hen's egg and have a richer yolk, making them sought-after for baking and eating. A small flock of six to eight ducks can supply more eggs than most households need through ten months of the year. A guide to breed choice, housing, daily management, and keeping production consistent.
sourceNotes: Lewis Wright, The Practical Poultry Keeper (1867), Internet Archive, duck chapters. DEFRA Poultry Code of Practice, Crown copyright. AHDB Pork / Poultry factsheets, open-access.

glossaryTerms.definition: A UK-developed laying duck breed producing up to 300 eggs per year. Light frame, calm temperament, suitable for a garden or smallholding flock.
A male duck. Drakes are not needed for egg production; an all-hen flock lays just as reliably without one.
The age at which a female duck or chicken is about to start laying. In Khaki Campbells this is around 17 to 20 weeks.

content.text: A flock of six Khaki Campbells will lay through most of the year, starting in their first February and continuing until October or November before moulting. They lay early in the morning, nearly always before 9 am, and they do not need a pond. A large drinker they can dip their bills into is enough.

is the standard laying breed for a UK smallholding. Indian Runners produce nearly as many eggs and take up less space, but they are more nervous and harder to handle. Aylesbury and Pekin are better for the table than the egg box. If eggs are the primary purpose, Khaki Campbell is the most practical choice.

Ducks sleep on the floor and do not perch. A simple shed or fox-proof house with a layer of straw on the floor is enough. Allow at least 0.4 square metres per bird inside. The house needs a ramp with horizontal grip strips; ducks manage a slope but struggle with smooth surfaces in the dark.

Ducks are wet birds and will make a mess of any housing quickly. Use a deep-litter system on a solid floor with wood shavings changed weekly, or make the floor slatted timber over a pit that can be cleared out. A solid concrete floor that slopes to a drain is ideal.

attrs.title: No pond needed
attrs.body: Ducks do not need a pond to stay healthy or to lay. They need water deep enough to dip their bills and submerge their heads for eye and nasal cleaning. A galvanised water trough kept full and cleaned every day is enough. Open ponds create wet ground around the run and become fouled rapidly in a small area.

content.text: Feed a waterfowl layer pellet if available, or a standard chicken layer pellet topped up with oyster shell. Ducks need niacin (Vitamin B3) in higher quantities than chickens; without enough they develop leg problems. Waterfowl-specific feed has the right levels. If using chicken pellets, add a small amount of brewer's yeast to the feed.

Ducks eat more than chickens of similar size. A laying Khaki Campbell needs around 170 to 200 g of pellets per day. Fill feeders in the morning and remove any uneaten wet feed each evening, as wet pellets ferment overnight and can cause crop and gut problems.

Keep hens confined until around 9 am. Ducks lay early but if they are let out before laying they will drop eggs across the run and garden instead of in the house. Once you know the last duck has laid, release them.

is not needed for egg production. An all-hen flock lays just as reliably and is more peaceful. If you want fertilised eggs for hatching, one drake to six or seven hens is enough; a higher ratio causes the hens to be over-mated and feather loss around the neck is common.

can be bought from waterfowl breeders in March or April. Expect to wait two to three weeks before the first egg as the flock settles into new housing. Production peaks around week six or eight and then levels at a consistent daily rate through summer.

items.symptom: Ducks laying across the run instead of in the house
items.cause: Released before all laying is complete, or house is too small or poorly sited
items.fix: Keep ducks confined until 9 am. Place a nest area with straw in the corner of the house; ducks will use the same spot repeatedly once they establish a habit.

items.symptom: Egg production drops sharply in autumn
items.cause: Day length shortening triggers the moult
items.fix: This is normal. Add a 40-watt bulb on a timer to bring light hours to 14 per day. Production usually resumes within two to three weeks. Do not light ducks under six months old.

items.symptom: Hens showing leg weakness, falling onto hocks
items.cause: Niacin deficiency, most common when fed chicken-only layer pellets
items.fix: Add brewer's yeast to feed at around 5 g per bird per day. Switch to a waterfowl-specific pellet. Recovery usually begins within one to two weeks with adequate supplementation.
